title: Placki ziemniaczane
subtitle: Polish potato pancakes, crisp-edged and yielding in the centre
excerpt: Grated potato, one egg, and a handful of flour pressed into thin cakes and fried until the edges catch and the middles stay soft. The Polish kitchen staple eaten with soured cream or fried onions.
sourceNotes: Based on standard Polish household tradition as documented in Maria Disslowa, 'Jak gotowac' (How to Cook, 1930s, Polish public domain) and cross-referenced with multiple twentieth-century Polish cookery manuscripts. The proportions follow the classic ratio of three medium potatoes to one egg and two tablespoons of flour.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The grated mixture will oxidise and darken if made ahead. Grate, season, and fry in the same session.

content.text: Placki ziemniaczane are the everyday potato pancakes of the Polish kitchen: raw grated potato mixed with egg and flour, seasoned, and fried flat in hot oil until the edges go dark and the centres stay soft. They take about 20 minutes to make from scratch and cost almost nothing. In Poland they appear at breakfast with a spoon of soured cream, at lunch as a side alongside bigos or fried sausage, and occasionally as a meal in themselves with a heap of goulash spooned over the top.

The main challenge is moisture. Potato holds a lot of water, and if you don't squeeze it out the pancakes steam rather than fry. Wring the grated potato in a clean cloth until no more liquid drips, then tip the potato into the bowl and proceed. The starch that settles in the squeezed-out liquid is worth keeping; pour the liquid off and scrape the white starch back into the mix.

A cast-iron skillet gives the best crust. Heat the oil until a shred of potato sizzles on contact, then work in batches of three or four. Crowding the pan drops the temperature and produces pale, soft pancakes instead of crisp ones.

items.prepNote: floury variety such as Maris Piper, peeled
finely grated or very finely chopped

content.text: A cast-iron skillet is the tool of choice; it holds heat and gives the pancakes the dark, lacework edge that defines them. A heavy stainless frying pan works but takes a little longer to get the colour right.

Coarsely grate the {{potato}} potato and the {{onion}} onion together into a mixing bowl. Gather the mixture in a clean cloth and squeeze hard over the sink until the dripping slows to occasional drops. Tip the squeezed mixture back into the bowl. Let the squeezed liquid sit for a minute; the white starch sinks to the bottom. Pour the liquid off carefully and scrape the starch back into the potato mixture.

Add {{eggs}} eggs, {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper-ground}} of ground pepper to the bowl. Mix thoroughly. The mixture should be just cohesive enough to hold a loose patty shape — not wet, not stiff.

Pour {{sunflower-oil}} of sunflower oil into the skillet and heat over a medium-high flame. To test the temperature, drop a small shred of potato mix into the oil; it should sizzle immediately. Spoon heaped tablespoons of the mixture into the pan and press each one to a rough circle about 8 cm across and 6 mm thick. Fry for 3–4 minutes until the base is deep golden and the edges are set. Flip once and cook for a further 3 minutes. Drain on a wire rack rather than paper; a rack keeps the undersides crisp.
Bring the pancakes to the table as each batch finishes. They soften quickly. Serve with a spoonful of {{creme-fraiche}} crème fraîche or soured cream alongside.

items.symptom: Pale, floppy pancakes that won't crisp
items.cause: Oil not hot enough, or too much moisture left in the potato
items.fix: Increase the heat before the next batch. Squeeze the mixture harder; if it still feels very wet, work in another tablespoon of flour.

items.cause: Too little flour or the mixture is too wet to bind
items.fix: Add a tablespoon of flour and mix again. Let the base set fully — a full 3–4 minutes — before attempting the flip.

items.cause: Heat too high or pancakes pressed too thick
items.fix: Reduce the heat slightly and press the mix to no more than 6–7 mm before frying.

items.cause: Oxidation from standing too long after grating
items.fix: Grate and fry in the same session. A small squeeze of lemon into the mix slows browning if a short wait is unavoidable.

content.text: A sweet version replaces the onion with a grated apple and serves the pancakes with jam. The savoury version pairs well with lightly fried sliced kielbasa, a mess of caramelised onions, or a fried egg on top. In some Polish households a little sauerkraut is folded into the mixture for a sharp note.

Potato pancakes appear across Central and Eastern Europe under many names (latkes in the Ashkenazi Jewish tradition, rösti in Switzerland, draniki in Belarus) but placki are the Polish form, and they have been a fixture of the Polish table since potatoes became a staple crop in the eighteenth century. They are weeknight food: cheap, quick, and satisfying. Street-food stalls at outdoor markets in Kraków and Warsaw still fry them to order, handed over in paper with a pot of soured cream on the side.
